1: YHWH's command to Moses for the second plague (frogs): 'Go to Pharaoh and say to him.' The repeated messenger-formula structures the plague sequence as a series of covenant lawsuits — YHWH states the case, makes the demand, announces the consequence. Each plague is a divine speech-act followed by divine action. This covenant-lawsuit structure anticipates the NT pattern of the gospel call: announcement, demand (repentance/faith), and consequence for refusal.
2: 'If you refuse to let them go, behold I will plague all your territory with frogs.' The frog plague targets the Nile again — Egypt's sacred river will now produce not fish or crocodiles but frogs in overwhelming numbers. The frog (tzfardea) was associated with the Egyptian goddess Heqet (frog-headed fertility goddess). YHWH turns Egypt's symbol of fertility and regeneration into an instrument of revulsion — the same reversal pattern as the Nile blood: what was life-giving becomes suffocating.
3: Frogs will come into Pharaoh's house, his bedroom, his bed — into the most intimate private spaces. The plague does not stay at the river but penetrates the most personal space of the king. Divine judgment is like this: it does not respect the boundary between public and private. Christ's coming judgment will similarly expose what is hidden — 'God will judge the secrets of men by Christ Jesus' (Romans 2:16). Nothing is off-limits to the scrutiny of the Creator who made the intimate as much as the public.
4: Frogs into the houses of servants, people, ovens, kneading bowls — the contamination extends from the palace to the kitchen, from the royal to the domestic. The frog plague did not discriminate socially: palace and peasant were equally invaded. This equality before divine judgment anticipates what Paul states about the cross: there is no distinction — 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God' (Romans 3:23). Judgment is comprehensively democratic before salvation becomes comprehensively available.
5: Aaron stretches out his hand with his staff over Egypt's waters — rivers, canals, ponds — and frogs come out to cover Egypt. The staff as the mediator's instrument of divine power connects all the plague signs into a unified sequence. The same staff that turned the Nile to blood now summons frogs. The instrument of Moses/Aaron is the embodied extension of YHWH's word — like the cross, which is the instrument by which divine judgment and divine mercy are both extended toward the world.
6: Aaron stretches out his hand and frogs cover the land. The plague achieves its full extent — every surface, every space. The comprehensiveness is part of the sign's argument: no space in Egypt is outside YHWH's jurisdiction. The NT makes the same claim about Christ's lordship: 'He who descended is the one who also ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things' (Ephesians 4:10). The plague fills Egypt to demonstrate YHWH's reach; the ascended Christ fills all things to demonstrate his lordship.
7: The magicians replicate the frog plague with their secret arts — more frogs appear. Again, the magicians intensify the judgment rather than alleviating it; they can add to the plague but cannot remove it. The great irony deepens: Egypt's own religious practitioners are making the situation worse. The religious systems that humans construct apart from YHWH tend toward this same irony — they multiply the burdens of law and ritual but cannot remove the fundamental problem of guilt before God (Hebrews 10:4).
8: Pharaoh calls Moses and Aaron: 'Pray to YHWH to take away the frogs — and I will let the people go to sacrifice.' This is the first instance of Pharaoh appearing to yield — the first use of prayer-language (hatiru) directed toward YHWH by the enemy. The reversal: the one who would not bow to YHWH's word now asks Moses to intercede. This foreshadows the eschatological acknowledgment when 'every knee will bow' (Philippians 2:10–11) — including those who resisted. The intercessor is always needed when judgment falls.
9: Moses offers Pharaoh the choice of timing: 'When shall I pray for you, your servants, and your people?' Moses does not assume the time — he gives Pharaoh the opportunity to name the hour of relief. This gesture of deference is remarkable: the mediator offers the offender the choice of timing. Christ's intercession similarly leaves room for the sinner to call on him: 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved' (Romans 10:13). The intercessor is available; the timing of the call is left to the one who needs deliverance.
10: Pharaoh says 'Tomorrow.' Moses: 'As you say — so that you may know there is no one like YHWH our God.' The frog plague will continue through the night before relief comes, so that the removal will be unmistakably supernatural. The purpose of the delay is knowledge: YHWH's incomparability (ein kaYHWH Eloheinu) will be demonstrated by the precision of the answer. The NT equivalent is the resurrection's timing — 'on the third day' (1 Corinthians 15:4) is not accidental but scheduled, so that the deliverance from death would be unmistakably divine.
11: The frogs will depart from the palace and its inhabitants and from the fields — and rest only in the Nile. Complete removal to a single location: judgment fully lifted from the land, confined to the river. The removal is as comprehensive as the plague. This is the pattern of final eschatological restoration: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore' (Revelation 21:4) — the reversal of plague is total, not partial.
12: Moses cries out (vayitzak) to YHWH concerning the frogs. The intercessory cry — the same root as Israel's cry in Egypt (Exodus 2:23) — is Moses performing for Egypt the very act that Israel performed for Israel. The deliverer intercedes for those he is delivering and even for those resisting deliverance. Christ's intercession on the cross ('Father, forgive them,' Luke 23:34) follows this same pattern: the mediator prays for those who have not yet turned, not only for his own people.
13: YHWH does according to Moses's word — the frogs die. Moses's prayer was effectual: YHWH acted 'according to the word of Moses' (kidvar Moshe). The intercessor's word before YHWH has operative force. This is the NT's theology of prayer through the mediator: Christ's intercession at the Father's right hand is always effectual (Hebrews 7:25 — 'he always lives to make intercession for them'). The frogs dying at Moses's word is a type of the authority of Christ's intercessory prayer.
14: The frogs were gathered into heaps (chamarim chamarim) — the land stank. Death followed deliverance: the removal of the plague left its residue in rotting heaps across Egypt. The smell of death lingered after the judgment was lifted. This is the sobriety of divine discipline: its aftermath is not immediately pleasant. Paul uses the same note in 2 Corinthians 7:10 — 'godly grief produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret.' The heaps of dead frogs are the grief that follows the moment of relief.
15: When Pharaoh saw there was relief (hakhafah*) he hardened his heart and would not listen. The classic post-relief relapse: the crisis passed, the urgency dissolved, the promise forgotten. This pattern — relief producing fresh hardness — is the most tragic form of spiritual regression. The letter to the Hebrews warns against the same dynamic: 'See to it that no one falls short of the grace of God' (Hebrews 12:15), not by remaining in crisis but by failing to keep the lesson of crisis past.
16: YHWH tells Moses: 'Say to Aaron, stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth so it becomes gnats throughout Egypt.' The third plague (gnats/lice, kinnim) requires no warning to Pharaoh — it comes without negotiation. The pattern shifts: the first two plagues were announced in advance; the third comes unannounced. This escalation signals that the window for pre-warning mercy has closed for this plague cycle. Divine patience has its own structure: there are moments when judgment follows without further announcement.
17: Aaron stretches out the staff and strikes the dust — dust becomes gnats on humans and animals throughout Egypt. The gnats come from the dust (afar) — the same substance from which humanity was formed (Genesis 2:7) and to which judgment returns it ('dust you are, and to dust you shall return,' Genesis 3:19). YHWH uses the humblest material — dust — to produce one of the most pervasive plagues. The cross similarly works through what the world considers base: 'We preach Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles' (1 Corinthians 1:23), yet this foolishness is the wisdom and power of God.
18: The magicians tried to replicate the gnats with their secret arts — and failed. They could not. This is the first failure of the magicians. Water-to-blood and frogs they could match; gnats from dust they could not. The boundary of counterfeit power has been reached: the imitation can go so far and no further. There is a point where false spiritual power is exposed as impotent — where the pretense of alternative authority simply fails. The cross is that point in ultimate terms: death could not hold the one who is Life (Acts 2:24).
19: 'This is the finger of God (etzba Elohim),' the magicians said to Pharaoh. The magicians' own confession: they recognize divine power at the point of their failure. Etzba Elohim — finger of God — will appear again in 31:18 as the inscription-method of the Sinai tablets. Luke 11:20 has Jesus say he casts out demons 'by the finger of God'; Matthew 12:28 renders this 'by the Spirit of God.' The Spirit who empowers Jesus's exorcisms is the same divine 'finger' the magicians encountered at their limit — the power that no counterfeit can imitate.
20: Pharaoh's heart was hardened and he did not listen — as YHWH had said. The refrain returns. The compounding of the phrase 'as YHWH had said' (ka'asher diber YHWH) is theologically precise: Pharaoh's hardening is not a surprise or a divine failure; it was announced in advance (Exodus 4:21; Exodus 7:3). The sovereignty of YHWH over Pharaoh's resistance becomes the vehicle through which YHWH's name is proclaimed throughout the earth (Romans 9:17). The resistance serves the revelation.
21: YHWH tells Moses: 'Rise early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh.' The fourth plague (flies) begins with YHWH taking the initiative — going to Pharaoh at his morning routine. The divine confrontation is persistent and patterned. The gospel's persistent announcement to a hostile world follows the same structure: 'Preach the word; be ready in season and out of season' (2 Timothy 4:2). The word of God is not deterred by prior refusals.
22: YHWH will set apart (hifleiti) the land of Goshen where Israel dwells — no swarms there. This is the first explicit distinction between Israel and Egypt in the plague sequence. From the fourth plague onward, YHWH places a boundary around his people that the judgment cannot cross. This separation is the OT form of what Paul calls being 'in Christ' — those joined to the covenant people are shielded from the wrath that falls on the world. 'God has not destined us for wrath, but to obtain salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ' (1 Thessalonians 5:9).
23: 'I will put a division (pedut) between my people and your people.' Pedut means separation/redemption — the same root as pidyon (ransom/redemption). The division between Israel and Egypt is itself an act of redemption: to be set apart from judgment is to be ransomed from it. Christ's work creates the same redemptive division: those in him are set apart from condemnation. 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1). The fourth plague's Goshen-distinction is the covenant type of this.
24: Heavy swarms of flies came into Pharaoh's house and the houses of his servants and throughout Egypt. But Goshen was spared. The contrast is enacted: the spatial boundary between judgment and protection is visible on the land. Two adjacent communities, one under plague and one untouched. The visibility of the distinction was itself part of the sign — it was not that Goshen escaped by chance but that YHWH specifically exempted it. The protection of God's people in the midst of judgment is always a visible testimony to his covenant faithfulness.
25: Pharaoh calls Moses and Aaron: 'Go, sacrifice to your God in the land.' The first concession — but it is a half-concession: sacrifice within Egypt, not a three-day journey into the wilderness. Pharaoh is willing to accommodate religion that stays within his territorial control. This is the perennial temptation for the church: to accept a form of Christianity that stays within the world's boundaries, that does not go out from Egypt into the wilderness of cross-bearing. Jesus refused the same compromise: 'My kingdom is not of this world' (John 18:36).
26: Moses refuses: what Israel must sacrifice would be 'an abomination to the Egyptians' — they would stone us. The sacrifices required by YHWH are offensive to Egypt's religion. This maps onto the NT offense of the cross: 'the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing' (1 Corinthians 1:18). The sacrifice YHWH requires — the death of his Son — is precisely what is most offensive to human religious expectation. It cannot be reduced to something the world will accept; it must go into the wilderness, outside the camp (Hebrews 13:13).
27: 'We must go three days' journey into the wilderness and sacrifice to YHWH our God as he commands us.' The three-day journey has long attracted theological attention — a journey into the wilderness that ends in sacrifice, followed by return. This three-day structure resonates with the resurrection: Jesus's journey into death (the wilderness of divine absence) for three days ends in the sacrifice completed and the return in resurrection. The pattern is embedded in the exodus demand before it is fulfilled in the cross.
28: Pharaoh says: 'I will let you go to sacrifice — only do not go very far away. Pray for me.' Another half-concession: you may go, but not far, and pray for me while you go. Pharaoh wants the benefits of Moses's intercession (relief from plagues) while maintaining control over the distance of Israel's departure. This is the condition of every half-hearted response to the gospel: seeking the benefits of Christ (comfort, forgiveness) while refusing the cost of full surrender (leaving Egypt, dying to self).
29: Moses promises to pray for the flies to depart — and warns Pharaoh not to deceive again in preventing Israel from going. The intercessor's prayer is conditional on the king's follow-through. Moses has been deceived before (implied) and names the risk explicitly. This is pastoral realism: intercessory prayer operates within the moral seriousness of covenant relationship. Intercession is not manipulation of God but honest engagement between the mediator, the petitioner, and YHWH.
30: Moses went out from Pharaoh and prayed to YHWH. The prayer follows the commitment — Moses does not wait to see if Pharaoh will follow through before interceding. Intercession precedes the evidence of the petitioner's response. This reflects Christ's intercession: he prays for us while we are still sinners (Romans 5:8), not after we have proven ourselves. The mediator acts first in faith that YHWH will respond and that the prayer will produce results in the one prayed for.
31: YHWH did according to Moses's word — the flies left, not one remained. The effectual prayer principle: YHWH acts 'according to the word of Moses,' just as in the frog plague (Exodus 8:13). The intercessor's word has force because YHWH honors the covenant relationship with his mediator. Christ's intercessory prayer is similarly effectual — 'I know that you always hear me' (John 11:42) — not because of the mediator's merit but because of the covenant relationship YHWH establishes with his anointed.
32: Pharaoh hardened his heart even this time — he did not let the people go. The cycle completes: plague, prayer, relief, and then renewed hardness. The relief given did not produce gratitude or change. This is the intractability of the unregenerate heart before divine grace: it can receive mercy, be briefly moved, and then return to its former position. The new covenant addresses this at the root: 'I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you' (Ezekiel 36:26). Without heart-transformation, external relief produces only temporary compliance.
